comment_text.sn11.22:2.7: That the speaker is Indra is confirmed by the vocative mārisa. Gods addressing Indra use bhaddantava.
comment_text.sn11.22:5.4: It’s notable not just how Sakka defuses the situation, but that he is full aware of how he does it, and uses his own conduct to inspire others.

root_text.sn11.22:1.3: “bhūtapubbaṁ, bhikkhave, aññataro yakkho dubbaṇṇo okoṭimako sakkassa devānamindassa āsane nisinno ahosi.
root_text.sn11.22:1.4: Tatra sudaṁ, bhikkhave, devā tāvatiṁsā ujjhāyanti khiyyanti vipācenti:
root_text.sn11.22:1.6: Ayaṁ yakkho dubbaṇṇo okoṭimako sakkassa devānamindassa āsane nisinno’ti.
root_text.sn11.22:1.7: Yathā yathā kho, bhikkhave, devā tāvatiṁsā ujjhāyanti khiyyanti vipācenti tathā tathā so yakkho abhirūpataro ceva hoti dassanīyataro ca pāsādikataro ca.
root_text.sn11.22:2.1: Atha kho, bhikkhave, devā tāvatiṁsā yena sakko devānamindo tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā sakkaṁ devānamindaṁ etadavocuṁ:
root_text.sn11.22:2.2: ‘idha te, mārisa, aññataro yakkho dubbaṇṇo okoṭimako sakkassa devānamindassa āsane nisinno.
root_text.sn11.22:2.3: Tatra sudaṁ, mārisa, devā tāvatiṁsā ujjhāyanti khiyyanti vipācenti:
root_text.sn11.22:2.5: Ayaṁ yakkho dubbaṇṇo okoṭimako sakkassa devānamindassa āsane nisinno”ti.
root_text.sn11.22:2.6: Yathā yathā kho, mārisa, devā ujjhāyanti khiyyanti vipācenti tathā tathā so yakkho abhirūpataro ceva hoti dassanīyataro ca pāsādikataro cāti.
root_text.sn11.22:2.7: So hi nūna, mārisa, kodhabhakkho yakkho bhavissatī’ti.
root_text.sn11.22:3.1: Atha kho, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo yena so kodhabhakkho yakkho tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā ekaṁsaṁ uttarāsaṅgaṁ karitvā dakkhiṇajāṇumaṇḍalaṁ pathaviyaṁ nihantvā yena so kodhabhakkho yakkho tenañjaliṁ paṇāmetvā tikkhattuṁ nāmaṁ sāveti:
root_text.sn11.22:3.3: Yathā yathā kho, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo nāmaṁ sāvesi tathā tathā so yakkho dubbaṇṇataro ceva ahosi okoṭimakataro ca.
root_text.sn11.22:3.4: Dubbaṇṇataro ceva hutvā okoṭimakataro ca tatthevantaradhāyīti.
root_text.sn11.22:3.5: Atha kho, bhikkhave, sakko devānamindo sake āsane nisīditvā deve tāvatiṁse anunayamāno tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imā gāthāyo abhāsi:

translation_text.sn11.22:1.3: “Once upon a time, mendicants, there was a native spirit who was ugly and deformed. He sat on the throne of Sakka, lord of gods.
translation_text.sn11.22:1.4: But the gods of the thirty-three complained, grumbled, and objected,
translation_text.sn11.22:1.6: This ugly and deformed spirit is sitting on the throne of Sakka, the lord of gods.’
translation_text.sn11.22:1.7: But the more the gods complained, the more attractive, good-looking, and lovely that spirit became.
translation_text.sn11.22:2.1: So the gods went up to Sakka and told him what had happened. Indra replied,
translation_text.sn11.22:2.7: ‘Surely, good fellow, that must be the anger-eating spirit!’
translation_text.sn11.22:3.1: Then Sakka went up to that spirit, arranged his robe over one shoulder, knelt with his right knee on the ground, raised his joined palms toward the anger-eating spirit, and pronounced his name three times:
translation_text.sn11.22:3.2: ‘Good fellow, I am Sakka, lord of gods! Good fellow, I am Sakka, the lord of gods!’
translation_text.sn11.22:3.3: But the more Sakka pronounced his name, the uglier and more deformed the spirit became,
translation_text.sn11.22:3.4: until eventually it vanished right there.
translation_text.sn11.22:3.5: Then Sakka, lord of gods, guiding the gods of the thirty-three, spoke this verse:
translation_text.sn11.22:4.2: I’m not easily drawn into the maelstrom.
translation_text.sn11.22:4.3: I don’t get angry for long,
translation_text.sn11.22:5.1: When I do get angry I don’t speak harshly,
translation_text.sn11.22:5.2: nor do I advertise my own virtues.
translation_text.sn11.22:5.4: out of regard for my own welfare.’”
